ankai: In Ankai relationships, one side gets continuously drained. Payment delays and scope explosions are the norm
Kai side 'finds everything beyond intolerable' — halfway through the project you're in the red but can't walk away
A bad year coinciding with Ankai means high risk of contract disputes or financial crisis
Vague contract terms are the biggest trap. Without quantified acceptance criteria, revisions become infinite
yusui: The Yu-side company unconsciously over-delivers, and margins get continuously squeezed
The Sui-side client treats your extra effort as standard service and won't voluntarily pay more
In a bad year, the Yu side's delivery capacity drops, previously free services become unsustainable, and client dissatisfaction spikes
Team burnout is the biggest risk. Key personnel may resign due to sustained overwork
kisei: Initial alignment costs are high and may consume all profit from the first project
Different terminology and work styles on both sides push communication costs to 2-3x expectations
The Ki side has a pressure tolerance ceiling. If alignment drags on too long, the team loses patience
mei: Commoditized collaboration lacks differentiation value. The client can switch vendors at any time
Weak contract renewal motivation. Once the client's internal team fills its capacity gap, you're surplus
Price competition becomes the only lever, and margins get crushed to the floor
gyotai: Great rapport leads to neglecting written documentation. No evidence trail when disputes arise later
The Gyo-side company gets absorbed in client needs and forgets its own team's rest and growth
Not recording verbal confirmations is the most common pitfall. Change management becomes a formality
